title: Trichotillomania (hair pulling disorder) - NHS
text:

Causes of trichotillomania It's not entirely clear what causes trich. It could be:
your way of dealing with stress or anxiety a chemical imbalance in the brain changes in hormone levels during puberty genetic - more than one person in a family may have the condition For some people, hair pulling can be a habit that's hard to break. The more they pull their hair out, the more they want to keep doing it.
When to see a GP See your GP if you're pulling your hair out or if you notice that your child is.
You should also see your GP if you or your child has a habit of eating hair. This can cause hairballs to form in the stomach, leading to serious illness.
Your GP may examine areas where the hair is missing to check that nothing else is causing the hair to come out, such as a skin infection.
If your GP thinks you have trich, you may be referred for a type of treatment called cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) .
If you are under 18, this may be offered through your local children and young people's mental health services .
Treating trichotillomania Trich is commonly treated using a type of CBT called habit reversal training.
This aims to help you replace a bad habit with something that's not harmful. Treatment usually involves:
keeping a diary of your hair pulling working out the triggers for your hair pulling an
